coredata.dc:title: PVDF/fluorescent carbon dots electrospun nanofibers with synergistic dipole alignment and multifunctional sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Sai, Liman
Ke, Xucheng
Cai, Yu
Dong, Shuyuan
Jia, Huixia
Cao, Liang
Wu, Zheng
Wang, Feifei
coredata.dc:description: The development of novel multifunctional materials has been attracting continuous attention in recent years. In this work, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with a designed quasi-molecular structure were introduced into polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofibers to realize electro-mechano-photoluminescence coupling effects. The N-H and O-H groups in the CDs formed hydrogen bonds with C-F groups in PVDF, which not only ensured the uniform arrangement of the CDs in the nanofiber matrix, but also facilitated the polarization of the PVDF. The β phase in PVDF was improved from 80% to 86% by doping of the CDs. Moreover, due to the asymmetric molecular structure of the carbon dot, it possessed an intrinsic dipole moment of 4.62 Debye. The self-assembly of the CDs on the nanofibers with a similar energy-favorable conformation improved the long-range spatial ordering in the nanocomposite. The CDs could also act as working dipoles, and contributed to the piezoelectric output of the whole device. Under a repeated finger imparting, the output voltage was enhanced from 6 V of the pure PVDF to 15 V of the nanocomposite. Furthermore, the CDs endowed the fiber with fluorescence capacity. The composite nanofiber exhibited bright fluorescence with excellent homogeneity. Through monitoring of the fluorescent intensity, visualized strain sensing and information encryption could be realized, which showed high potential of the nanofiber as a multifunctional nanocomposite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
PVDF
Piezoelectric
Photoluminescence
Nanofibers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhanced electrical properties and β-phase content of the PVDF by CDs
Molecular interaction between the CDs and PVDF
Piezoelectric performance of the PVDF/CDs nanocomposite device
Multifunctional applications of the fluorescent composite film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11974250 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-vis and PL spectra of the isolated CDs (excited at 365 nm). Inset: structure of the fluorescent IPCA molecule [24]. (b) PL spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelength.

(a) Scheme of the electrospinning process. Inset: the PVDF/CDs precursor solution under daylight and 365 nm excitation. (b) Photograph of the pure PVDF (left) and PVDF/CDs (right) films under daylight (up) and 365 nm excitation (down). (c) Laser scanning confocal microscope image of the PVDF/CDs film (405 nm excitation).
SEM (a, b) and TEM (c, d) images of the PVDF/CDs nanofibers.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of the pure PVDF and PVDF/CDs nanofibers.

(a) Scheme of the interaction between the CDs and PVDF. (b) FTIR spectra of PVDF and PVDF/CDs nanofibers in the 3200-3600 cm−1 region. High-resolution XPS scans of C1s (c) and F1s (d) of PVDF and PVDF/CDs nanofibers.
Output voltage of the pure PVDF and PVDF/CDs composite nanofiber films with different doping amount of CDs.
(a) Scheme of the conformation of the molecular CDs and the calculated dipole moment. (b) Optimized adsorption configuration of a single CDs molecule on the PVDF polymer chain.

(a) PL spectra of the CDs in solution (black curve), pure CDs film (grey curve) and PVDF/CDs film (red curve). (b) PL spectra of five points on the PVDF/CDs film. Inset: Fluorescent intensity measured at different points as a function of film thickness.
β-phase content in PVDF with different amount of CDs.
Piezoelectric coefficient (d 33) and relative dielectric constant (ε r) in PVDF with different amount of CDs.

head.ce:title: PVDF/fluorescent carbon dots electrospun nanofibers with synergistic dipole alignment and multifunctional sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Material and Device, Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Material and Device

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Optoelectronic Material and Device, Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMOE Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of novel multifunctional materials has been attracting continuous attention in recent years. In this work, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with a designed quasi-molecular structure were introduced into polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofibers to realize electro-mechano-photoluminescence coupling effects. The N-H and O-H groups in the CDs formed hydrogen bonds with C-F groups in PVDF, which not only ensured the uniform arrangement of the CDs in the nanofiber matrix, but also facilitated the polarization of the PVDF. The β phase in PVDF was improved from 80% to 86% by doping of the CDs. Moreover, due to the asymmetric molecular structure of the carbon dot, it possessed an intrinsic dipole moment of 4.62 Debye. The self-assembly of the CDs on the nanofibers with a similar energy-favorable conformation improved the long-range spatial ordering in the nanocomposite. The CDs could also act as working dipoles, and contributed to the piezoelectric output of the whole device. Under a repeated finger imparting, the output voltage was enhanced from 6 V of the pure PVDF to 15 V of the nanocomposite. Furthermore, the CDs endowed the fiber with fluorescence capacity. The composite nanofiber exhibited bright fluorescence with excellent homogeneity. Through monitoring of the fluorescent intensity, visualized strain sensing and information encryption could be realized, which showed high potential of the nanofiber as a multifunctional nanocomposite.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, flexible piezoelectric sensors, actuators, and energy harvesters have attracted great attention due to their high flexibility, high sensitivity and diverse geometries [1,2]. As one of the popular light weight polymer, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) has been extensively applied to flexible devices due to its electro-activity, biocompability and flexibility [3–5]. Accelerometers, vibrometers and orientational sensors with high performance were also demonstrated with attractive mechanical properties, which allowed their applications in human motion monitoring and robotics [6,7].

With the development of integration and miniaturization of the flexible piezoelectric devices, smart multifunctional sensors have stimulated great scientific and technological interests. In the previous works, electro-mechano-optical conversion was achieved in a Pr-doped system which combined the piezoelectric and electroluminescent effect [8]. An electric field-induced strain response as well as the photoluminescence (PL) enhancement was reported in a rare-earth ion modified piezoelectric composites [9]. A reversible tuning of the PL of a ferroelectric single crystal via external strain was also realized [10]. These multifunctional inorganic composites showed great potential in smart device applications. On the contrary, most of the present PVDF-based devices focus on the electro-mechanical coupling, while multifunctional effects were rarely reported. Nanofillers such as metal nanoparticles [11], carbon nanotubes [12] and inorganic ceramic [13] were widely used to construct PVDF-based nanocomposites. However, the nanofiller in the PVDF matrix was usually limited to a supporting material, which greatly hindered the developing of multifunctional nanocomposites with high performance.

Here, carbon dots (CDs) with a quasi-molecular structure, which was reported to improve the piezoelectric output in previous works [14–18], were used as the nanofiller to realize electro-mechano-photoluminescence coupling effects. Self-poled PVDF/CDs composite nanofibers were obtained by electrospinning. The N-H and O-H groups in CDs could form hydrogen bonds with -CF2 groups in PVDF, and facilitated the dipole alignment in PVDF to realize high content of β-phase. Moreover, the well-arranged CDs could also act as working dipoles, and exhibit piezoelectricity under external stress. Under the optimized condition, the composite film showed an output voltage of 15 V under pressure of approximately 1 N at 5 Hz with an effective area of 1 cm2. Owing to the high homogeneity of the CDs in the nanofibers, visualized fluorescent sensing and information encryption were realized in the proposed multifunctional nanocomposite fibers.

Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (Mw=700000) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sigma. Citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), acetic ether and acetone were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Deionized water was used as the solvent for synthesis and purification of the CDs.

Typically, CA and EDA with a molar ratio of 1:1 were dissolved in deionized water, and stirred thoroughly to reach a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 140°C for 10 h. After the reaction, the obtained dark brown solution was cooled naturally to the room temperature and concentrated by evaporation. A large amount of acetic ether was added to extract the CDs from the water phase. A colorless solution with strong blue fluorescence was obtained. Finally, acetic ether was evaporated, and the CDs powder was re-dispersed in DMF for further use.

A mixed 10 mL solution of DMF/acetone containing different amount of the CDs was prepared by keeping the ratio of DMF : acetone at 6:4. Then, 1 g PVDF was added, and the mixed solution was heated to 80°C and stirred for 2 h. Then the solution was cooled to the room temperature, further stirred for 12 h and used as the electrospin precursor solution. The electrospinning was operated at a rate of 1.0 mL/h−1 with an applied voltage of 18 kV, and a distance between the spinneret and collector of 15 cm.

The piezoelectric nanogenerator was fabricated with top and bottom conducting carbon electrodes (size: 1×1 cm2) attached on either side of the films. Electrical output leads were attached to each of the carbon electrodes. The electrode-PVDF-electrode stack was encapsulated with PDMS for enhancing its robustness and stability. PDMS was prepared from Sylgard 184 by mixing base and curing agents in the ratio of 10:1. Vacuum drying was used to eliminate air bubbles, and the colloid was heated at 70°C for 8 h. To eliminate the effect of triboelectric signals produced between the human finger and device surface, an ultrathin PDMS film was stuck to the finger when tapping. It is known that, objects of the same material generate negligible triboelectric charges when contacting each other. The output voltage (Voc) was recorded by an oscilloscope.

The morphological structures of the nanofibers were examined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, INSPECT F50) operated at 20 kV, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200 kV. The crystalline phases of PVDF were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy in a transmission mode (FT-IR) (Shimadzu, FTIR-8400S) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis by a Bruker, D8 Advance. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out by a VG ESCALAB 220i-XL surface analysis system. UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Lambda 950 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-6310PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Columbia, MD, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) observations were carried out with a Nikon A1R apparatus. For electrical measurements, gold electrodes were deposited on both sides of the composite films using a sputter coater. The d 33 piezoelectric constant of the nanofibers was analyzed by d 33 piezoelectric coefficient measuring instrument (ZMpiezo-B). Permittivity was measured using an impedance analyzer (Aglient 4294A) at room temperature.

The dipole moment of the CDs was calculated using the Gaussian 16 suite of programs [19]. The geometry of the studied molecule was optimized at the B3LYP-D3BJ/aug-cc-pVDZ level of theory. Frequency analysis was performed to confirm that we have obtained stable structures on the potential energy surfaces. The dipole moment of the molecule was calculated at the same level.

The geometrical optimization and binding energy calculations based on first-principles theory were carried out Gaussian 16 package. An octamer of vinylidene fluoride was built to represent for the polyvinylidene fluoride. The B3LYP functional and the aug-cc-pvdz basis set were applied [20]. The dispersion force between molecules were corrected by DFT-D3 method.

It is widely accepted that, the as-prepared carbon dot is a combination of carbon core (sp2-hybridized π domains), surface groups and individual organic fluorophores [21,22]. Especially, in the reaction system containing citric acid and ethylenediamine, an identified fluorophore called (imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo-, IPCA) was reported by Song et al. in 2015 [23]. This fluorescent molecule could be isolated from the products of CDs with careful purification. The identified molecular structure of IPCA is shown in the inset picture in Fig. 1a . In this work, the CDs were synthesized and purified according to the reference [23], and the fraction of the molecular IPCA with bright blue fluorescence was collected. Fig. 1a shows the UV-vis and PL spectra of the IPCA. The peak positions were consistent with the reference, and the excitation-independent property (Fig. 1b) indicated its character of a molecular fluorophore [24]. Considering that the fluorophores were isolated from a mixed solution, and may not appear to be precisely monodispersed, the isolated IPCA in this work is still called the quasi-molecular CDs.

Fig. 2 shows the scheme of the electrospinning process. The PVDF/CDs precursor solution showed a yellow color under daylight, and a transparent blue light under 365 nm excitation. Fig. 2b shows the photograph of the electrospun nanofiber films. Under daylight, both the pure PVDF and PVDF/CDs films showed white color. Under 365 nm excitation, the PVDF/CDs film showed a bright blue fluorescence, which can also be discovered in the LSCM image (Fig. 2c). The homogeneous fluorescence of the composite film indicated uniform dispersion of the CDs in the PVDF matrix.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM and TEM images of the PVDF/CDs nanofibers. The nanofibers had a diameter of 150-300 nm (Fig. 3a). In the TEM images (Fig. 3c, d), a thin layer at the side of the nanofiber can be discovered, indicating the adsorption of the CDs. As discussed above, the CDs in this work were more close to micro-molecules but not nanoparticles. It is reasonable that, the CDs on the PVDF nanofibers will appear as a continuous layer rather than individual nanospheres. While aggregation and random distribution of the nanofillers were always found in the composite system, here the small sizes of the CDs facilitated their uniform adsorption on the PVDF nanofibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced electrical properties and β-phase content of the PVDF by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline phase of the PVDF can be characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 4a , the peak at 18.3° belonged to the α-phase, the peaks at 20.6° and 36.2° belonged to the β-phase [25,26]. The sharp peak at 20.6° indicated dominance of β-phase in the PVDF nanofiber, which showed the nanofibers were efficiently polarized during electrospinning. With increasing of the CDs amount, the peak of α-phase at 18.3° was weakened, indicating suppression of the α-phase of the PVDF by CDs doping.

FTIR is another useful method to determine the crystal phase of the PVDF [27]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the 765 and 976 cm−1 peaks which corresponded to the α-phase [28,29] of the PVDF were obviously suppressed by doping of the CDs. Meanwhile, the 1280 cm−1 peak of β-phase was enhanced with increasing amount of the CDs. The FTIR spectra clearly showed the polarization effect of the PVDF by the CDs.

Specific bands such as 765 and 840 cm−1 have been used to represent the α and β phase of PVDF, respectively. The β phase content of the PVDF can be calculated using eq 1 and 2:
(1)
F
(
β , r
)
=
A 840
(
K β
/
K α
)
A 765
+
A 840
(2)
F
( β )
=
A 1280
A 1280
+
A 1234
where Aα and Aβ are the absorbance value at 765 and 840 cm−1, respectively, Kα and Kβ are the absorption coefficients at the respective wavenumber. Kα = 6.1×104 cm2/mol−1, Kβ = 7.7×104 cm2/mol−1 [30,31]. The calculation details are shown in Table 1 . During electrospinning, the PVDF chain turned to the TTTT configuration due to high electric field and mechanical stretching. The doping of the CDs further facilitated the orientation of the dipoles in the PVDF, and resulted in a high content of β phase.

The relative permittivity (ε r) and piezoelectric coefficient (d 33) properties of the composite films are shown in Table 2 . Both ε r and d 33 increased continuously with the CDs concentration increasing in the doping concentration range. It is widely accepted that, ε r in composite materials is related to the interfacial polarization. Charge accumulation at the interfaces between CDs and PVDF could increase the dielectric permittivity of the nanocomposite. Besides, the CDs with permanent dipole moment might also contribute to the enhancement of ε r in the nanocomposite [32]. This is also consistent with the FTIR results where higher content of β phase was obtained with higher CDs doping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular interaction between the CDs and PVDF
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, when PVDF and CDs were co-dispersed in DMF, the C-F bond in PVDF could interact with the N-H and O-H groups in CDs to form hydrogen bonds (C-F···H-N) [33,34]. As shown in Fig. 5b , a broad peak at 3400-3500 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of the PVDF nanofiber appeared with CDs doping, indicating existence of the O-H/N-H groups. In the C1s XPS spectra in Fig. 5c, a peak of C-H bond at 283.0 eV appeared with adding of the CDs. In the F1s spectra (Fig. 5d), the peak position shifted to lower binding energy with CDs doping, suggesting the electronic environment of the CF2 groups in PVDF has changed. The FTIR and XPS spectra provided evidence for the molecular interaction between PVDF and CDs. In the precursor solution and during the electrospinning, both the -OH and -NH groups in CDs could form hydrogen bonds with -CF2 groups in PVDF (Fig. 5a). This molecular force rotated F atoms to the same side of the carbon chain in PVDF, and promoted the alignment of the -CF2 dipoles to reach a continuous β-phase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Piezoelectric performance of the PVDF/CDs nanocomposite device
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical stress can induce piezoelectric charges, which results in a potential difference between the two electrodes. In order to equilibrate the induced potential in the composite film, external free charges will move to the electrodes, and a positive signal can be detected. When the stress is released, the accumulated charges will flow back and generate a negative electrical signal. Here, the PVDF and PVDF/CDs films with 30 μm thickness were integrated with top/bottom electrodes (working area of 1 cm2), and tested by repeated finger tapping. The pressure was approximately 1 N measured by a force sensor, and the frequency was kept at ≈5 Hz. As shown in Fig. 6 , the pure PVDF film showed an output voltage of 6 V, which could be continuously enhanced with increasing amount of CDs. With 1.6% CDs doping, the Voc of the composite reached 15 V, which was 2.5 times higher than the pure PVDF film.

As discussed above, the β-phase content in PVDF could be improved by polarization effect of the CDs through molecular interaction. It is noted that, with 1.2% and 1.6% CDs doping, the β-phase in PVDF was almost the same (both 86% in Table 1). However, the PVDF with 1.2% CDs showed a Voc of 12 V, while the PVDF with 1.6% CDs showed a Voc of 15 V. Another reason might be responsible for improving of the output voltage besides the crystalline structure change in PVDF.

Here, we propose a possible mechanism to explain the experiment results. DFT calculation was used to measure the dipole moment of a single molecular CDs (Fig. 7a ). According to the computational result, the CDs possessed an intrinsic dipole moment of 4.62 D. Therefore, the carbon dot itself can be treated as a dipole. Moreover, an optimized conformation of the CDs adsorbed on the PVDF was also simulated by DFT (Fig. 7b). This configuration illustrates the state of the lowest adsorption energy of the CDs. Hydrogen bond is rotatable, and the orientation of the CDs will spontaneously turn to an energy-favorable state. It is reasonable to assume that, in the polymer matrix, the CDs with same molecular structure will tend to adsorb on the PVDF chain via a similar energy-favorable configuration. The large amount of CDs dipoles with arranged orientation may also exhibit piezoelectricity. The PVDF nanofibers with the attached CDs can be treated overall as a composite of giant crystal-like structures. The enhanced spatial ordering in the nanocomposite can increase the piezoelectric response of the whole device.

Several groups have also considered the contribution of the nanofillers as aligned dipoles which can cause piezoelectric effect. For example, Liu et al. [35] reported that, surface polar groups of the carbon dots could be orientated with external electric field polarization, and exhibited piezoelectric signal. Also, breaking of the centrosymmetry of graphene oxide in PVDF could enhance the piezoelectric property of the composite film [36]. Isakov et al. demonstrated that [37], organic 2-methyl-4-nitroaniline (MNA) incorporated in electrospun poly (L-lactic acid) could show a single-crystal like orientation with their dipole moments aligned with the fiber axis. The strong electric filed and the minimized elastic energy was responsible for this oriented alignment. Here, the improved piezo-response of the nanocomposite can be considered as a combination of the change in dipole moment of PVDF itself, and the change in dipole moment of the arranged non-centrosymmetric CDs attached to PVDF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multifunctional applications of the fluorescent composite film
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the specific molecular structure of the CDs ensured their strong interaction with PVDF. This molecular interaction resulted in a highly homogeneous distribution of the CDs on the nanofibers, which can also be observed by their uniform fluorescence (Fig. 2b). Generally, for fluorescent organic molecules, their fluorescence can be greatly changed in different assembled states. Here, we used a transmission mode to measure the PL spectra of the fluorescent film. A 365 nm light source was placed under the film, and the photoluminescence of a certain point on the film was detected from above. The focal spot size of the spectrometer was 200 μm. A pure CDs film was also prepared by dropping the CDs solution onto a filter paper and drying at 80°C. As shown in Fig. 8a , the pure CDs film (grey curve) had a widened and red-shifted PL spectrum, which was due to partial aggregation of the nanoparticles. However, when the CDs were introduced into PVDF polymer matrix, their spectra remained a narrow peak width (red curve), which showed their high dispersity with enough distance between the particles. The emission peak of the CDs in PVDF shifted to a shorter wavelength compared with that in solution. This might be attributed to the molecular interaction between PVDF and CDs, which influenced the electronic states in the CDs.

Owing to the high homogeneity of the CDs in the composite film, a fluorescent self-detection of the film thickness can be realized. As a proof-of-concept experiment, a 1.6% doping PVDF/CDs film with a thickness gradient was prepared (Movie S1 shows a simple strategy to obtain a fibrous film with a gradient thickness). Eight different points on the film were selected, and their PL spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 8b, the fluorescence at different points became weaker with the decreasing of the film thickness, which was due to less amount of the CDs. A linear correlation existed within the range of film thickness from 32 to 83 μm ((R2 = 0.987). The detection limit was determined to be 250 nm. While it is always difficult to calculate the thickness of an ultra thin film, this experiment shows a possible visual detection method. Further investigations will be carried out in the future work.

Beside the thickness sensing, a dynamic strain sensing can also be realized. Movie S2 shows the changing of the fluorescent intensity of the composite film under a tiny stretching. The tensile deformation of the film could hardly be recognized, but the spectrum revealed this change. It is noted that, the fluorescence was measured by a reflection mode in case of the strain sensing. The fluorescent intensity dropped when stretching the film, which might due to the energy transfer between the more closely packed CDs. The sensitive changing of the fluorescent signal with the deformation showed the potential for the composite film as a high-resolution strain sensor.

Based on the sensitive response of the fluorescent intensity with the film thickness, here, an interesting information encryption application was designed and demonstrated. We collected a PVDF/CDs fibrous film using a designed two layers of aluminum foil on the roller (Figure S1) to obtain film with different thickness (the word 'shnu' in the PVDF/CDs film will have a thickness of about 60 μm, while in other places the film thickness is 90 μm). This slight difference can hardly be recognized by naked eyes. When UV light source was placed above the PVDF/CDs film, the whole film still exhibits a uniform blue light. However, when the light source is placed beneath the film, which means a transition detecting mode of the fluorescence of the film, the letters of 'shnu' can be clearly recognized (see Movie S3). This interesting information encryption effect shows potential application of the nanocomposite in many fields.

In this work, carbon dot with a quasi-molecular structure was synthesized and used as a doping material in the PVDF nanofiber composite. The ultra-small sizes of the CDs allowed their uniform dispersion in the polymer matrix. The chemical groups in CDs could interact with PVDF to form hydrogen bonding, and facilitate the alignment of the -CF2 dipoles in PVDF. Moreover, with asymmetric molecular structure and oriented arrangement on the nanofiber, the carbon dots could also act as working dipoles to contribute to the output of the composite. Finally, taking advantage of the high homogeneity of the CDs in the polymer matrix, a visualized strain sensing was realized due to amplified fluorescence change when stretching the film. Moreover, owing to the high sensitivity of the fluorescence with the film thickness, a unique information encryption application was demonstrated. This work provided the possibility of achieving synergistic spatial arrangement in a nanocomposite, which might pave the way for the development of multifunctional electronic devices by new ways of thinking.

Liman Sai: Experiments, writing. Xucheng Ke: d 33 and ε r measurements. Yu Cai: Encapsulation of the piezoelectric device. Feifei Wang: Conceptualization. Liang Cao & Zheng Wu: Spelling and grammar checking. Shuyuan Dong & Huixia Jia: Set up of the spectrum device.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11974250).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apmt.2023.102025.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced power output of an electrospun PVDF/MWCNTs-based nanogenerator by tuning its conductivity

L. Lu, W. Ding, J. Liu, B. Yang, Flexible PVDF based piezoelectric nanogenerators, Nano Energy, 78 (2020) 105251. 10.1016/j.nanoen.2020.105251.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-sensitivity acoustic sensors from nanofibre webs
